intro:
Ordering the right amount of concrete is essential — too little means a second batch that may not match, and too much wastes money while creating disposal problems. Our concrete calculator converts your project dimensions into cubic yards (how concrete is ordered from ready-mix trucks) and cubic feet (for mental math), and also shows how many 60-lb and 80-lb bags of pre-mixed concrete you would need for small pours.

Concrete is ordered in cubic yards for ready-mix deliveries (minimum order typically 1 cubic yard). For smaller projects, bags of pre-mixed concrete (just add water) are more practical. A standard 80-lb bag makes 0.60 cubic feet of concrete; a 60-lb bag makes 0.45 cubic feet.

Always add 10% to your calculated volume for spillage, forms, and to avoid running short. For slabs, the standard thickness is 4 inches for patios and walkways, 6 inches for driveways with vehicle traffic.
howItWorks: Enter the project length and width in feet and depth in inches. The calculator converts depth to feet, multiplies all three dimensions, then converts to cubic yards (÷27) and cubic feet. It also shows how many 60-lb and 80-lb bags of pre-mix you would need to achieve the same volume.
interpretationGuide: Cubic yards is the standard unit for ordering ready-mix concrete. Cubic feet is useful for manual calculations. Bags 60-lb and bags 80-lb show the retail bag equivalent if you prefer bagged concrete for smaller pours. Always add 10% to your order.

faqs.question: How thick should a concrete slab be?
faqs.answer: Residential patio/walkway: 4 inches thick. Driveway (car traffic): 4–6 inches. Driveway with truck/RV traffic: 6 inches. Garage floor: 4 inches with rebar. Structural slab (load-bearing): 6+ inches with rebar, designed by engineer. Enter thickness in inches in the depth field.

faqs.question: How do I calculate concrete for a circular area?
faqs.answer: Area = π × radius². For a 10-foot diameter circle: radius = 5 ft, area = 3.14159 × 25 = 78.5 sq ft. Multiply by depth in feet for cubic feet, then divide by 27 for cubic yards. The rectangular room calculator is not suitable for circles — use this formula manually.

faqs.question: When should I use ready-mix concrete vs. bags?
faqs.answer: Bags (pre-mix): for volumes under 1 cubic yard (projects under 50 sq ft at 4 inches). Ready-mix: for larger pours. One cubic yard = 27 cubic feet. A 10×20 ft driveway at 4 inches thick = 200 sq ft × 0.333 ft = 66.7 cu ft = 2.47 cu yards — order 3 yards from a ready-mix truck.

faqs.question: What is the minimum ready-mix concrete order?
faqs.answer: Most ready-mix concrete companies have a minimum order of 1 cubic yard, with short-load surcharges for orders under 3–5 cubic yards. For projects needing 1–5 cubic yards, compare the cost of ready-mix (including truck fee) versus renting a mixer and buying bags.

faqs.question: Does concrete need rebar or wire mesh?
faqs.answer: Standard residential patios and walkways often use wire mesh (6×6 W1.4×W1.4 welded wire mesh) or no reinforcement. Driveways benefit from rebar (½" diameter) or fiber mesh added to the mix. Slabs subject to freeze-thaw cycles or vehicle loads should always use rebar on 18-inch centers.

faqs.question: How long does concrete take to cure?
faqs.answer: Concrete reaches walking strength in 24–48 hours, vehicle strength in 7 days, and design strength (3,000+ psi) in 28 days. Rapid-setting mixes cure faster but should not be used for large pours. Curing properly (keeping concrete moist for 7 days) is as important as mix quality.

faqs.question: What PSI concrete do I need for my project?
faqs.answer: 3,000 PSI: most residential patios, walkways, steps. 3,500 PSI: driveways, garage floors. 4,000 PSI: heavy vehicle traffic, exposed aggregate. 4,500+ PSI: structural/commercial applications. Standard bagged concrete is typically 3,000–4,000 PSI; specify when ordering ready-mix.

faqs.question: How do I prevent concrete cracks?
faqs.answer: Use proper mix (not too wet — add no more water than specified). Install control joints every 8–12 feet to guide cracking. Use wire mesh or rebar. Cure properly. Ensure proper base preparation (4-inch compacted gravel sub-base). Avoid pouring in temperatures below 40°F or above 90°F without special precautions.

faqs.question: How much does concrete cost?
faqs.answer: Ready-mix: $125–$200 per cubic yard (prices vary by region and admixtures). 80-lb bags: $5–$7 each (0.60 cu ft each). A 10×10 patio at 4 inches = 1.23 cubic yards = $155–$250 ready-mix, or 55 bags = $280–$385 in bags. Ready-mix is usually more economical for pours over 1 cubic yard.

faqs.question: What is slump and why does it matter for concrete?
faqs.answer: Slump measures concrete workability — how much a standard cone of concrete drops when the mold is removed. Standard residential slump: 4–5 inches. Higher slump = more water = weaker concrete. Ordering concrete at the right slump (not adding water on site) is critical for achieving specified strength.
